note:
公司今天推出隐身，在Menlo Ventures的一轮上筹集了1550万美元的种子资金，并从真正的企业参与。 UC卫生和科罗拉多大学创新基金占一轮500万美元，其他天使包括GoEhealth Brandon Cruz和麦迪逊工业Larry Gies。在投资前三年谈到Sinha的Menlo Greg Yap将加入董事会。
   同性恋者也创立了Ai-Assistant Viv的Kittlaus表示，他开始思考在接受自己的严重健康问题后如何在数字健康方面进行差异。 2016年，Kittlaus被诊断出患有胰腺神经内分泌癌，苹果首席执行官史蒂夫乔布斯的癌症相同类型的癌症。
 “我花时间研究它的想法，但我错过了我以前的两家公司的事情，这是一些惊人的技术创新，可以形成一个你可以移动世界的楔子，”他说。
 Kittlaus在过去的夏天，与他的朋友肖恩卡罗兰肖恩卡罗兰提到了这次内部对话。作为SIRI第一个投资者的朋友将他介绍给Tuhin Sinha，这家曾多年开发过习惯于电力RIVA的技术的科学家。
 要使用RIVA，所有人都需要做的是在手机上打开应用程序，然后点击“GO”，触发手机背面的相机闪光灯。该应用程序然后将用户指导将手指放在右相机上，并帮助它们调整定位，直到它锁定到位。之后，RIVA将使用光跟踪血压变化并在屏幕上创建渲染。
  “这项技术的众所周知的一部分在血管上闪耀着光芒，并摇晃着它，”Sinha说。 “新奇是波浪的形状，它如何与血压有关，我们的秘密酱正在寻找那些波莎更改并以严谨而全面的方式验证它们。” Sinha拒绝分享他们的验证是如何验证的，但是它是一个创业公司在各种不同场景中测量血压的关键 - 思考站立或坐姿 - 看它是有效的。
 一旦Riva追踪五到七个心跳数据，它就在那一刻对某人的血压全面了解。 
然后，如果存在风险，从高血压开始，可以将是HIPAA符合HIPAA符合的数据，以便分析。
 “像智能手机一样搬到一个平台正在动员[健康和疾病管理]的测量和管理，”Sinha说。 RIVA Health是一种纯粹的软件解决方案。
 该公司目前正在使用Android手机验证其软件，但Kittalus表示，“任何现代手机都应该能够获得所需的信号”。
 任何健康技术公司，特别是RIVA的大障碍是它是否可以获得FDA批准临床使用。该公司目前与FDA进行该进程，并询问今年夏天出来的免费应用程序的用户参与审判和数据收集以加强其批准过程。
 目前，RIVA正在使用其技术来跟踪临床环境中血压的变化，第二半将在家庭环境中。通过跟踪其在现实世界中的系统中，它可以证明它在实验室和家庭设置中，有助于其疾病管理技术是有效的。
 “有很多Gizmos和小工具将声称血压读数，”Kiittlaus说。 “我称之为血压作为一种新奇，在那里它给你血压读数而不是诊所。”例如，一项研究表明，Fitbit确实履行血压，但始终低估了数字。其他解决方案如苹果手表或无齿状可穿戴物测量非临床使用的血压，这意味着它不是超级准确，只能通知明显的血压问题。
 RIVA希望每天和精确地被医生依赖，这是其整体赚钱的一部分。虽然团队不会分享任何公布的研究或证明其科学方法，但教皇的首席创新官员博士说，它是“防弹”技术。过去三年超过700家公司试图与Zane的团队合作，RIVA是遇到酒吧的少数人之一。 
“当我们的团队测试它时，它真的在第一次出售方案中，基本上从未发生过，”Zane说。他补充说，进入最大的障碍之一是人们过去需要设备，RIVA带来了“一种实际工作的新技术，这将嵌入患者已经携带的东西并允许他们管理他们的心脏疾病或高血压。“
 “公司的核心产品是医疗结果，”Sinha说。该公司是初创公司浪潮的一部分，相信基于结果的医疗保健是未来，这是医生支付结果的模型，而不是他们在一天内完成的访问数量。有了这一愿景，RIVA计划通过向医院系统和提供商销售成果来通过销售成果来限制：如果它可以提供帮助医生的工具，让人们竭尽全力，并表明早期的问题，它可以制定一个稳固的论证，为什么系统应该采用它。
 只有20％的医疗保健在基于价值的模型上工作，因此即使具有正确的情绪，这将是一个障碍。与此同时，Kittlaus表示，它正在与保险公司合作，以支付其服务。 RIVA将获得报销治疗和管理高血压。
 “我们希望为消费者提供免费，免费为医生免费，保险将涵盖它，”他说。
 如果和当FDA清除这项技术时，Kittlaus表示，医生和医学们“仍然会对它持怀疑态度”，但最终将相信结果更准确，并且正在进行，而不是袖口。
 “你在寻找一个应用程序，”他说。 “而不是医学。”
 该应用程序待审批，将于今年晚些时候或明年初提供。接下来的几个月为RIVA将是确定其成功和有效性的关键 - 以及首席科学家的SINHA表示这将是严格的，但快速。他对公司的成功有个人领导。 
僧伽在59岁之前失去了五个兄弟，一个妹妹，他的父亲到了心脏病。 现在，他的应用程序能够跟踪使他首先失去这些家庭成员的情况。  “我觉得我胸前有一个滴答时间炸弹，”他说。 “如果有的话，我要为自己做这件事。”
note_en:
The company, launching out of stealth today, has raised $15.5 million in seed funding in a round led by Menlo Ventures, with participation from True Ventures. UC Health and University of Colorado Innovation Fund accounted for $5 million of the round, with other angels including GoHealth’s Brandon Cruz and Madison Industries Larry Gies. Greg Yap of Menlo, who talked to Sinha for three years before investing, will be joining the board.
   Kittlaus, who also founded AI-assistant Viv, says that he began thinking about how to make a difference in digital health after undergoing his own severe health issues. Kittlaus was diagnosed with pancreatic neuroendocrine cancer in 2016, the same type of cancer that late Apple CEO Steve Jobs died from.
 “I spend time researching ideas on it, but I was missing the thing that I’ve had in both my previous companies, which was some amazing technical innovation that could form a wedge that you can move the world with,” he said.
 Kittlaus mentioned this internal conversation with his friend,  Shawn Carolan, this past summer. The friend, who was the first investor in Siri, introduced him to Tuhin Sinha, the scientist who spent years developing the technology that is used to power Riva.
 To use Riva, all a person needs to do is open the app on their phone and tap ‘Go’, which triggers the camera flash on the back of the phone. The app will then guide the user to place their finger over the right camera, and help them adjust positioning until it locks into place. After that, Riva will use the light to track blood pressure change and create a rendering of it on screen.
  “The well-known part of this technology is shining a light on a blood vessel and getting a wave out of it,” Sinha said. “The novelty is the shape of the wave, how it relates to blood pressure, and our secret sauce is looking at those waveshape changes and validating them in a rigorous and comprehensive way.” Sinha declined to share how they are validating exactly, but said that it is key that a startup has to measure blood pressure in a variety of different scenarios – think standing or sitting – to see if it is effective.
 Once Riva tracks five to seven heartbeats worth of data, it has a comprehensive understanding of someone’s blood pressure at that moment.
 The data, which is HIPAA compliant, can then be sent to a family physician or doctor’s office to be analyzed if a risk is present, starting with hypertension.
 “Moving to a platform like the smartphone is mobilizing the measurement and management of [health and disease management],” Sinha said. Riva Health is a purely software solution.
 The company is currently in the process of verifying its software with Android phones, but Kittalus says that “any modern phone should be able to acquire the signal needed” to work.
 A big hurdle for any health tech company, and especially Riva, is whether it can get FDA approval for clinical usage. The company is currently engaged in that process with the FDA, and will ask users who pilot its free app, coming out this summer, to participate in the trial and data-gathering to bolster its approval process.
 Right now, Riva is using its technology to track the changes in blood pressure in a clinical setting, and the second-half will be in a home setting. By tracking the use of its system in the real world, it can prove that it works in a laboratory and home set-up, helping it prove that its disease management technology is effective.
 “There are a lot of gizmos and gadgets that will claim to do blood pressure reading,” Kiittlaus said. “I call it blood pressure as a novelty where it gives you blood pressure reading but not a clinic.” For example, a Fitbit does track blood pressure but consistently underestimates the number,  a study says. Other solutions like the Apple Watch or cuffless wearables measure blood pressure for non-clinical usage, which means that it isn’t super accurate and only notifies obvious blood pressure problems.
 Riva wants to be daily and precise enough to be relied on by doctors, which is part of its overall route to make money. While the team wouldn’t share any published research or proof about its scientific method, Dr. Richard Zane, the chief innovation officer of UCHealth said that it is “bulletproof” technology. Over 700 companies in the past three years have tried to work with Zane’s team, and Riva is one of the few that met the bar.
 “When our team tested it, it actually worked out of the box the first time, which basically never happens,” Zane said. He added that one of the biggest barriers to entry is that people have needed devices in the past, and Riva brings “a novel technology that actually works that will be embedded in something that patients already carry around with them and allow them to manage their heart disease or hypertension.”
 “The core product of the company is healthcare outcomes,” Sinha said. The company is part of the wave of startups that believe outcome-based healthcare is the future, a model where doctors are paid for results instead of the number of visits they complete in a day. With this vision, Riva plans to monetize by selling outcomes to hospital systems and providers: if it can provide a tool to help doctors keep people out of surgery and indicate issues earlier than before, it can make a solid argument as to why systems should adopt it.
 Only 20% of healthcare works on the value-based model, so this will be a hurdle even with the right sentiment. In the meantime, Kittlaus says that it is working with insurers to pay for its service. Riva would get reimbursed for treating and managing hypertension.
 “We want to keep it free for the consumer, free for the doctor, and insurance will cover it,” he said.
 If and when the FDA clears this technology, Kittlaus says that doctors and medics “will still be skeptical about it” but will ultimately be convinced of the outcomes being more accurate, and ongoing, than the cuff.
 “You’re prescribing an app,” he said. “Instead of medicine.”
 The app, pending FDA approval, will be available for public late this year or early next year. The next few months for Riva will be key in determining its success and validity – and Sinha, the chief scientist, say it will be rigorous, but fast. He has a personal tie to the company’s success.
 Sinha has lost five brothers, one sister, and his father before the age of 59 to heart disease. Now, his app has the ability to track the condition that made him lose these family members in the first place.
 “I feel like I have a ticking time bomb in my chest,” he said. “And if anything, I’m going to do this for myself.”
title: Riva Health希望将智能手机转变为血压监测器
title_en: Riva Health wants to turn your smartphone into a blood pressure monitor

note:
经过艰难的一年的遥远和人的学校教育，许多学生，老师和他们的家人感到从大流行学习中烧毁。但是，将数字学习工具与学校的公司正在享受冠状病毒风险收获。
 根据CB Insights的一份报告，追踪初创企业和风险投资的公司报告，我们在2019年的481亿美元起，促进教育技术初创企业的风险和股票融资已增加一倍多。
 根据英国市场研究公司FutureSource Consulting的数据，在同一时期，美国运送给美国中小学和中学的平板电脑的数量与1400万增加到2670万。
 “我们已经看到了一个真正的需求爆炸，”Futuresource高级市场分析师Michael Boreham说。 “这是一种巨大的，大量的海洋变化。”
 但随着学校和风险资本家的数十亿美元的更多地区，即学校和风险投资者沉入教育技术即将进行测试。一些远程学习服务，如视频会议，可能会看到他们的学生受众竞争。
 “明年肯定会发生震动，”Newsela首席执行官Matthew Gross表示，学校的流行阅读课程应用程序。 “我一直在称之为”伟大的技术紧缩“。”
 然而，即使是ED技术市场合同，行业高管也会说没有回头。他们说，大流行加速了笔记本电脑和学习应用程序的传播，他们说，为数百万教师，学生及其家人规范化数字教育工具。 
“这使得在1997年共同创立的Blackboard的资深Ed-Tech Entrepreneur说：”这使得通过五到10年来采用技术在教育中采用技术在教育中采用技术。 “你不能在网上教育中培训数十万名教师以及数百万学生，并没有期望有深刻的影响。”
 科技福音传教士长期预测计算机将改变教育。学习的未来，许多承诺，涉及由人工智能提供动力的应用程序，这些应用程序会比他们的人类教师更快，更精确地调整儿童能力的教训。
 这种机器人教学革命在即将到来的情况下，部分是因为很少有学习应用程序表明他们显着提高了学生的结果。
  相反，在大流行期间，许多学校简单地转向视频会议等数字工具，以在线转移传统实践和日程安排。批评者表示，推动偏远学生的学校日，只有许多面临着家庭大流行挑战的儿童的差异。
 “我们将再次在我们的一生中看到一个更强大的教育系统保守主义的演示，”马萨诸塞州理工学院助理教授贾斯汀·莱科斯说，在线学习和最近写的这本书“未能破坏：为什么技术独自无法改变教育。“
 在线互动的应用程序正在报告非凡的增长，投资者遵循。
 CB Insights在最大的交易中说：Zuoybang，一个中国ED-Tech巨头，为幼儿园的学生提供现场在线课程和家庭作业帮助，通过12年级，投资者总共增加了23.5亿美元的投资者，包括阿里巴巴和红杉国库国。 
中国辅导初创的袁菲大约，腾讯等投资者总共增加了35亿美元。来自数百万教师使用的挪威的测验应用程序，最近从SoftBank筹集了大约2.15亿美元的测验。
 在美国，最近最大的ED-Tech交易涉及初创企业，帮助教育工作者在线提供教育和等级课程或举行课堂讨论。其中包括Newsela和HardPod，这是许多教师用来创建直播互动视频课程或者在虚拟实地考察中获取学生的应用程序。
  “特别是在K-12中，在师生之间的伙伴之间的伙伴jennifer Carolan表示，这是众多学习，这是一位专注于已经投资于附近和Newsela的教育的合作伙伴。 “我们对这些产品令人兴奋的是真正延长了课堂教师的能力。”
  在大流行前，一些ED-Tech初创公司报告记录增长具有大量的学校受众。然后去年春天，随着学区转向远程学习，许多教育应用程序袭击了一个普遍的大流行增长战略：他们暂时为学生其他剩余的教师提供了优质的服务。
 “来自巨大的采用展开的是什么，”Owl Ventures的董事总经理Tory Patterson表示，该公司是一家投资教育初创公司，如Newsela等教育初创公司。他说，一旦学年结束，他说，ED-Tech初创公司开始努力将学区转换为付费客户，并“我们看到了非常广泛的加息供应。”
 截至12月底，学校在Newsela支付了1100万学生账户，从2019年增加了约87％。上个月，启动宣布它已筹集了1亿美元。现在，Newsela价值价值10亿美元，这是一个里程碑，可能是Instacart和送达等消费者应用程序，但旨在为美国公立学校的教育应用程序仍然比较少见。
 附近还报告了指数增长。在自由拍摄视频课程应用之后，启动将其用户基础飙升到去年年底的巨额飙升至120万教师 - 超过2019年的五倍跳跃。上个月，附近宣布它已同意由文艺复兴时期收购将学校销售学校的公司，以6.5亿美元。 
尽管青少年体育运动爆发，但许多专家，父母和参与者认为，益处超过了风险。
 一年中第一次回到学校是什么样的？学生分享他们的第一天乱码。
 一些为学校提供免费服务的消费者科技巨头也获得了福利，获得了观众份额，并获得了数百万学生习惯使用他们的产品。
 例如，谷歌课堂的全球观众，谷歌的免费课程和评分应用程序，已经飙升到超过1.5亿的学生和教育工作者，从去年初从4000万美元起。和缩放视频通信称，它在大流行期间提供了免费服务，在25个国家超过125,000所学校。
 但是，教师是否依赖于远程学习的工具可以维持他们的受欢迎程度将遵循应用程序在教室里有用。
  对于一个人来说，Newsela在教育工作者中致力于其灵活性。该应用程序让他们选择主题新闻文章或短篇小说的类讨论，不同版本的文本取决于学生的阅读级别。 Newsela首席执行官Gross先生表示，该应用程序还向教师提供了对每个孩子的进步的快速反馈，提醒他们对可能需要注意的学生是在线或在课堂上。
 “教师开始意识到一个物理和远程教室真正建立哪些工具，”克罗斯先生说，“这两种设置都同样很好地工作。” 
PEP Carrera表示，近距离视频课程应用程序，也希望在学校维持学校的牵引力。在大流行期间，教育工作者，如休斯顿地区的八年级科学老师，在应用程序上使用了在应用程序上的功能来调查学生，创建测验或要求学生使用绘图工具来绘制太阳系 - 工作的数字工具 - 数字工具对于Live Cradiach和远程指令。
 “无论他们在哪里，我都可以向所有学习者播放课程，”哈罗德女士说，他同时教会亲自和偏远的学生。
 她的一个投诉：她不能在附近的时间存放超过几个教训，因为她的学校没有买单。 “它仍然昂贵，”她说。
 在ZOOM的情况下，教育的未来不太清楚，这些服务是为商业用途而设计的，由学校脱离大流行的必要性而设计。
 在电子邮件中，Zelly Steckelberg Zoom的首席财务官表示，她预计教育机构将投资“几乎沟通的新方法”超越远程教学 - 例如使用父母教师协会会议，学校董事会会议和家长教师会议使用缩放。
 Chasen先生，Ed-Tech Entrepreneur，正在依靠它。他最近创立了课堂技术，这是一个在线课程管理工具的初创公司，如出勤率和分级特征 - 用于教育工作者和公司培训师在放大上持有现场课程。该公司已从投资者筹集了4600万美元，包括Bill Tai，最早的放大支持之一。
 “我没有带来一些新的高级A.I.方法论，“Chasen先生说他的视频教室的新应用程序。 “你知道老师需要什么？他们需要能够在课堂上发出工作，给出测验和等级。“
note_en:
After a tough year of toggling between remote and in-person schooling, many students, teachers and their families feel burned out from pandemic learning. But companies that market digital learning tools to schools are enjoying a coronavirus windfall.
 Venture and equity financing for education technology start-ups has more than doubled, surging to $12.58 billion worldwide last year from $4.81 billion in 2019, according to a report from CB Insights, a firm that tracks start-ups and venture capital.
 During the same period, the number of laptops and tablets shipped to primary and secondary schools in the United States nearly doubled to 26.7 million, from 14 million, according to data from Futuresource Consulting, a market research company in Britain.
 “We’ve seen a real explosion in demand,” said Michael Boreham, a senior market analyst at Futuresource. “It’s been a massive, massive sea change out of necessity.”
 But as more districts reopen for in-person instruction, the billions of dollars that schools and venture capitalists have sunk into education technology are about to get tested. Some remote learning services, like videoconferencing, may see their student audiences plummet.
 “There’s definitely going to be a shakeout over the next year,” said Matthew Gross, the chief executive of Newsela, a popular reading lesson app for schools. “I’ve been calling it ‘The Great Ed Tech Crunch.’”
 Yet even if the ed-tech market contracts, industry executives say there is no turning back. The pandemic has accelerated the spread of laptops and learning apps in schools, they say, normalizing digital education tools for millions of teachers, students and their families.
 “This has sped the adoption of technology in education by easily five to 10 years,” said Michael Chasen, a veteran ed-tech entrepreneur who in 1997  co-founded Blackboard, now one of the largest learning management systems for schools and colleges. “You can’t train hundreds of thousands of teachers and millions of students in online education and not expect there to be profound effects.”
 Tech evangelists have long predicted that computers would transform education. The future of learning, many promised, involved apps powered by artificial intelligence that would adjust lessons to children’s abilities faster and more precisely than their human teachers ever could.
 That robotic teaching revolution has been slow in coming, in part because very few learning apps have shown they significantly  improve students’ outcomes.
  Instead, during the pandemic, many schools simply turned to digital tools like videoconferencing to transfer traditional practices and schedules online. Critics say that push to replicate the school day for remote students has only exacerbated disparities for many children facing pandemic challenges at home.
 “We will never again in our lifetime see a more powerful demonstration of the conservatism of educational systems,” said  Justin Reich, an assistant professor at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology who studies online learning and recently wrote the book “ Failure to Disrupt: Why Technology Alone Can’t Transform Education.”
 Apps that enable online interactions between teachers and students are reporting extraordinary growth, and investors have followed.
 Among the biggest deals, CB Insights said: Zuoyebang, a Chinese ed-tech giant that offers live online lessons and homework help for students in kindergarten through 12th grade,  raised a total of $2.35 billion last year from investors including Alibaba and Sequoia Capital China.
 Yuanfudao, another Chinese tutoring start-up, raised a total of  $3.5 billion from investors like Tencent. And  Kahoot, a quiz app from Norway used by millions of teachers, recently raised about $215 million from SoftBank.
 In the United States, some of the largest recent ed-tech deals involved start-ups that help educators give and grade assignments, lead lessons or hold class discussions online. Among them are Newsela and Nearpod, an app that many teachers use to create live interactive video lessons or take students on virtual field trips.
  “Especially in K-12, so much of learning is sparked through dialogue between teachers and students,” said Jennifer Carolan, a partner at Reach Capital, a venture capital firm focused on education that has invested in Nearpod and Newsela. “We are excited about these products that are really extending the capabilities of the classroom teachers.”
  A number of ed-tech start-ups reporting record growth had sizable school audiences before the pandemic. Then last spring, as school districts switched to remote learning, many education apps hit on a common pandemic growth strategy: They temporarily made their premium services free to teachers for the rest of the school year.
 “What unfolded from there was massive adoption,” said Tory Patterson, a managing director at Owl Ventures, a venture capital firm that invests in education start-ups like Newsela. Once the school year ended, he said, ed-tech start-ups began trying to convert school districts into paying customers, and “we saw pretty broad-based uptake of those offers .”
 By the end of December, schools were paying for 11 million student accounts on Newsela, an increase of about 87 percent from 2019. Last month, the start-up announced that it had  raised $100 million. Now Newsela is valued at $1 billion, a milestone that may be common among consumer apps like Instacart and Deliveroo but is still relatively rare for education apps aimed at American public schools.
 Nearpod also reported exponential growth. After making the video lesson app free, the start-up saw its user base surge to 1.2 million teachers at the end of last year — a fivefold jump over 2019. Last month, Nearpod announced that it had agreed to be  acquired by Renaissance, a company that sells academic assessment software to schools, for $650 million.
 Despite  outbreaks in youth sports, many experts, parents and participants believe that the benefits outweigh the risks.
 What’s it like to  go back to school for the first time in a year? Students share their first-day jitters.
 Some consumer tech giants that provided free services to schools also reaped benefits, gaining audience share and getting millions of students accustomed to using their product.
 For instance, the worldwide audience for Google Classroom, Google’s free class assignment and grading app, has skyrocketed to  more than 150 million students and educators, up from 40 million early last year. And Zoom Video Communications says it has provided free services during the pandemic to more than 125,000 schools in 25 countries.
 But whether tools that teachers have come to rely on for remote learning can maintain their popularity will hinge on how useful the apps are in the classroom.
  Newsela, for one, has gained a devoted following among educators for its flexibility. The app lets them choose topical news articles or short stories for class discussion, with different versions of the text depending on a student’s reading level. Mr. Gross, Newsela’s chief executive, said the app also provided quick feedback to teachers on each child’s progress, alerting them to students who might need attention whether they are online or in the classroom.
 “Teachers are starting to realize which tools are really built for both a physical and a remote classroom,” Mr. Gross said, “that work equally well in both settings.”
 Nearpod, the video lesson app, also expects to maintain traction in schools, said Pep Carrera, the start-up’s chief executive. During the pandemic, educators like  Nesi Harold, an eighth-grade science teacher in the Houston area, have used features on the app to poll students, create quizzes or ask students to use a drawing tool to sketch the solar system — digital tools that work for both live classroom and remote instruction.
 “It allows me to broadcast the lesson to all of my learners, no matter where they are,” said Ms. Harold, who simultaneously teaches in-person and remote students.
 Her  one complaint: She can’t store more than a few lessons at a time on Nearpod because her school hasn’t bought a license. “It’s still pricey,” she said.
 The future in education is less clear for enterprise services, like Zoom, that were designed for business use and adopted by schools out of pandemic necessity.
 In an email, Kelly Steckelberg, Zoom’s chief financial officer, said she expected educational institutions would invest in “new ways to virtually communicate” beyond remote teaching — such as using Zoom for Parent Teacher Association meetings, school board meetings and parent-teacher conferences.
 Mr. Chasen, the ed-tech entrepreneur, is counting on it. He recently founded Class Technologies, a start-up that offers online course management tools — like attendance-taking and grading features — for educators and corporate trainers holding live classes on Zoom. The company  has raised $46 million from investors including Bill Tai, one of the earliest backers of Zoom.
 “I’m not coming up with some new advanced A.I. methodology,” Mr. Chasen said of his new app for video classrooms. “You know what teachers needed? They needed the ability to hand out work in class, give a quiz and grade it.”
title: CB Insights：教育技术初创公司在2020年全球筹集了12.58亿美元，2019年的4.81亿美元; 随着人的教学回报，远程服务可能会看到使用下降
title_en: CB Insights: education tech startups raised $12.58B globally in 2020, up from $4.81B in 2019; as in-person teaching returns, remote services may see usage drop

鼠标胚胎看起来非常正常。他们所有的器官都按预期开发，以及他们的四肢和循环和神经系统。他们的微小的心在每分钟的正常170次跳动中跳动。
 但这些胚胎在母老鼠中没有生长。科学家们第一次在人工子宫内开发，在人工子宫内，第一次壮举，科学家们在周三报道。
 在以色列的Weizmann科学研究所的实验旨在帮助科学家了解哺乳动物如何发展以及基因突变如何，营养和环境条件可能会影响胎儿。但是，有一天的工作可能会提高关于其他动物是否应该在谋生子宫之外培养的深刻问题。
 在本研究中公布的本质上，雅各布汉纳博士描述了在妊娠五天内从小鼠的子宫中除去胚胎，并在人工子宫中生长六天。
 此时，胚胎通过其发展中途是大约一半的;完全妊娠期大约20天。在这个发展阶段的人类将被称为胎儿。迄今为止，汉娜博士和他的同事通过这种方式增长了1000多个胚胎。
 “这真的是一个显着的成就，”Paul Tesar是西方储备大学医学院的发展生物学家。
 柏林Max Planck分子遗传学研究所的基因组法规总监Alexander Meissner表示，“越来越令人惊叹”，这项研究是“主要里程碑”。 
但研究已经超出了论文中描述的研究人员。在接受采访时，哈纳博士说，他和他的同事们在施肥后从女性老鼠的输卵管中占据了受精卵 - 在第0天的发展中 - 并在人工子宫中生长了11天。
 到目前为止，研究人员能够从实验室中的哺乳动物中施肥鸡蛋，并仅在短时间内生长它们。胚胎需要谋生的子宫。 “胎盘哺乳动物在子宫锁定，”Tesar博士说。
 “发展生物学的圣杯是要了解单细胞，一种受精卵，可以使人体中的所有特定细胞类型成长为40万亿细胞，”Tesar博士说。 “自从时间开始，研究人员一直在努力制定回答这个问题的方法。”
 研究组织和器官的发展的唯一方法是转向蠕虫，青蛙和苍蝇等物种，不需要子宫，或者在不同时间从实验动物的子宫中去除胚胎，提供更像快照的瞥见而不是视频。
 所需要的是进入子宫内部，在哺乳动物中观察和调整发展的方法。对于汉娜博士，这意味着开发一种人工子宫。
 他花了七年来开发一个两件系统，包括孵化器，营养和通风系统。小鼠胚胎置于培养箱内的玻璃小瓶中，在那里它们漂浮在特殊的营养液中。
   小瓶连接到慢慢旋转的轮子上，所以胚胎不附着在墙壁上，在那里它们会变形并死。孵化器连接到通风机，该通风机为胚胎提供氧气和二氧化碳，控制这些气体的浓度以及气体压力和流速。 
在发展的第11天 - 只以上半鼠怀孕 - 汉娜博士和他的同事们检查了胚胎，只有苹果种子的大小，并将它们与生活小鼠的子宫中的那些进行了比较。科学家发现，实验室胚胎相同。
 然而，到那个时候，实验室生长的胚胎变得太大而无法在没有血液供应的情况下存活。他们有一个胎盘和蛋黄袋，但通过扩散给它们的营养溶液不再足够了。
 哈哈博士在接受采访时说，越过障碍是下一个目标。他正在考虑使用富含富含胚胎的胎盘的人工血液供应。
 与此同时，实验招手。汉娜博士说，将胚胎活着和妊娠中途保持活跃和发展中途的能力“是我们的金矿。”
 人工子宫可能让研究人员了解更多关于为什么怀孕结束在流产中的妊娠或为什么受精卵未植入。它打开了一个新的窗口，达到基因突变或缺失如何影响胎儿发育。研究人员可以观察各个细胞迁移到其最终目的地。
 该作品是“突破”，卡特赫省生物学和生物工程教授Zernicka-Goetz说。它“在实验鼠标模型中打开了新的学习发展的新时代。”
  最近的发展提供了另一个机会。研究人员直接从小鼠成纤维细胞 - 结缔组织细胞中产生了小鼠胚胎 - 使早期胚胎制成早期胚胎，而不从受精卵开始。 
梅斯纳博士说，将该开发与汉娜的工作与汉娜的工作博士的工作相结合，“现在你不需要小鼠胚胎发展。”科学家们可能能够使他们需要的所有胚胎都能从结缔组织中进行。
 如果科学家可以在没有施肥的情况下制造胚胎，并且可以在没有子宫的情况下研究他们的发展，但梅斯特纳博士说，“你可以远离胚胎毁灭。”没有必要在学习过程中施肥小鼠鸡蛋以摧毁它们。
 但工作可能最终延伸超越老鼠。周三出版的另外两篇论文在周三报告了以这种方式在创造早期人类胚胎附近的尝试。当然，Meissner博士说，人类胚胎的创造是几年之岁 - 如果它完全允许。目前，国际法规禁止在施肥14天内学习人类胚胎。
 在未来，Tesar博士说：“我们可能有能力开发人类胚胎从施肥中兴起的能力完全在子宫外。”
 当然，即使是这种科幻场景的建议必然会吓唬许多人。但它是早期的，没有保证人类胎儿可以完全在子宫之外发展。
 甚至假设他们可以，Tesar博士指出，“这是合适的是伦理学家，监管机构和社会的问题。”

The mouse embryos looked perfectly normal. All their organs were developing as expected, along with their limbs and circulatory and nervous systems. Their tiny hearts were beating at a normal 170 beats per minute.
 But these embryos were not growing in a mother mouse. They were developed inside an artificial uterus, the first time such a feat has been accomplished, scientists reported on Wednesday.
 The experiments, at the Weizmann Institute of Science in Israel, were meant to help scientists understand how mammals develop and how gene mutations, nutrients and environmental conditions may affect the fetus. But the work may one day raise profound questions about whether other animals, even humans, should or could be cultured outside a living womb.
 In a study  published in the journal Nature, Dr. Jacob Hanna described removing embryos from the uteruses of mice at five days of gestation and growing them for six more days in artificial wombs.
 At that point, the embryos were about halfway through their development; full gestation is about 20 days. A human at this stage of development would be called a fetus. To date, Dr. Hanna and his colleagues have grown more than 1,000 embryos in this way.
 “It really is a remarkable achievement,” said Paul Tesar, a developmental biologist at Case Western Reserve University School of Medicine.
 Alexander Meissner, director of genome regulation at the Max Planck Institute for Molecular Genetics in Berlin, said that “getting this far is amazing” and that the study was “a major milestone.”
 But the research has already progressed beyond what the investigators described in the paper. In an interview, Dr. Hanna said he and his colleagues had taken fertilized eggs from the oviducts of female mice just after fertilization — at Day 0 of development — and had grown them in the artificial uterus for 11 days.
 Until now, researchers were able to fertilize eggs from mammals in the laboratory and grow them for only a short time. The embryos needed a living womb. “Placental mammals develop locked away in the uterus,” Dr. Tesar said.
 “The holy grail of developmental biology is to understand how a single cell, a fertilized egg, can make all of the specific cell types in the human body and grow into 40 trillion cells,” Dr. Tesar said. “Since the beginning of time, researchers have been trying to develop ways to answer this question.”
 The only way to study the development of tissues and organs was to turn to species like worms, frogs and flies that do not need a uterus, or to remove embryos from the uteruses of experimental animals at varying times, providing glimpses of development more like snapshots than video.
 What was needed was a way to get inside the uterus, watching and tweaking development in mammals as it happened. For Dr. Hanna, that meant developing an artificial uterus.
 He spent seven years developing a two-part system that includes incubators, nutrients and a ventilation system. The mice embryos are placed in glass vials inside incubators, where they float in a special nutrient fluid.
   The vials are attached to a wheel that slowly spins so the embryos do not attach to the wall, where they would become deformed and die. The incubators are connected to a ventilation machine that provides oxygen and carbon dioxide to the embryos, controlling the concentration of those gasses, as well as the gas pressure and flow rate.
 At Day 11 of development — more than halfway through a mouse pregnancy — Dr. Hanna and his colleagues examined the embryos, only the size of apple seeds, and compared them to those developing in the uteruses of living mice. The lab embryos were identical, the scientists found.
 By that time, though, the lab-grown embryos had become too large to survive without a blood supply. They had a placenta and a yolk sack, but the nutrient solution that fed them through diffusion was no longer sufficient.
 Getting past that hurdle is the next goal, Dr. Hanna said in an interview. He is considering using an enriched nutrient solution or an artificial blood supply that connects to the embryos’ placentas.
 In the meantime, experiments beckon. The ability to keep embryos alive and developing halfway through pregnancy “is a gold mine for us,” Dr. Hanna said.
 The artificial womb may allow researchers to learn more about why pregnancies end in miscarriages or why fertilized eggs fail to implant. It opens a new window onto how gene mutations or deletions affect fetal development. Researchers may be able to watch individual cells migrate to their ultimate destinations.
 The work is “a breakthrough,” said Magdalena Zernicka-Goetz, professor of biology and biological engineering at Caltech. It “opens the door to a new age of studying development in the experimental mouse model.”
  A recent development provides another opportunity. Researchers have directly created mouse embryos from mouse fibroblasts — connective tissue cells — making early embryos without starting with a fertilized egg.
 Combine that development with Dr. Hanna’s work, and “now you don’t need mice to study mouse embryo development,” Dr. Meissner said. Scientists may be able to make all the embryos they need from connective tissue.
 If scientists could make embryos without fertilizing eggs and could study their development without a uterus, Dr. Meissner said, “you can get away from embryo destruction.” There would be no need to fertilize mouse eggs only to destroy them in the course of study.
 But the work might eventually extend beyond mice.  Two other papers  published in Nature on Wednesday report on attempts that edge near creating early human embryos in this way. Of course, Dr. Meissner said, creation of human embryos is years away — if it is permitted at all. And for now, international regulations prohibit studying human embryos beyond 14 days of fertilization.
 In the future, Dr. Tesar said, “it is not unreasonable that we might have the capacity to develop a human embryo from fertilization to birth entirely outside the uterus.”
 Of course, even the suggestion of this science fiction scenario is bound to horrify many. But it is early days, with no assurance human fetuses could ever develop entirely outside the womb.
 Even assuming they could, Dr. Tesar noted, “whether that is appropriate is a question for ethicists, regulators and society.”
title: 科学家在机械子宫中生长小鼠胚胎
title_en: Scientists Grow Mice Embryos in a Mechanical Womb
